sentences.text: OK.
It's the 12th of July, 1996.
The survivor is Selien Abram.
Interviewer is Mary Ziegler.
We're in Sydney, New South Wales, Australia.
And the language this morning is English.
Go.
Selien, S-E-L-I-E-N.
Abram, A-B-R-A-M.
Koperberg.
K-O-P-E-R-B-E-R-G.
No.
Walsh.
W-A-L-S-H.
Lein.
L-E-I-N.
Yeah.
Nearly 75.
[LAUGHS]
Amsterdam.
Holland.
No.
I was an only child.
My father's name was Philip Koperberg.
And my mother was Miethe Prins.
Maiden name.
We were wholesalers in textile.
Yeah.
No.
Fantastic.
Oh, it was a terrific city.
And-- and it was a good life there, very good.
Very big.
We were not only Jews.
I never went to Jewish school or anything.
But there was a big Jewish community.
They killed 125,000.
Well, you knew you were Jewish.
And sometimes, you had people who were nasty, like at one stage I had a girl at school who called me a bloody Jew.
And I chased her.
And it was Christmastime and she had plaits.
And I was hanging on her plaits.
I let my feet off the ground.
And they called me the Christmas tree.
[LAUGHS].
Honest.
So look, there were always people who were anti-Semitic.
But for the rest no, we never had any troubles.
But did you identify as a Jew?
Did you go-- did you observe the high holidays, things like that?
No.
No.
The only thing we observed was Friday night.
We had special dinners on Friday night.
And if there were more people in the family, they came together.
But that's about as far as it went.
No.
No.
Well, when I was about three years old, we moved to a shop.
We had a textile shop and a baby shop next door.
And we lived behind the shop.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And then when I was, oh--
Oh, that was [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the corner of [INAUDIBLE].
Two rooms, a lounge, a kitchen, and two bedrooms.
That was it.
I slept on the textile.
And when I looked up at the cupboard, was textile, everywhere.
[LAUGHS] That's how I grow up.
So--
No.
Not there.
No, later we moved to a flat, when my father aspired to wholesale.
And there we had a shower made in a cupboard, because there was none there.
It was not in those days.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] from there, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I got married in '41.
Yeah.
I went to school.
Well, I first went to normal school.
I don't know how you call it.
No.
And the second school was an home science school by the name of fondet, D-E-T.
Cooking, cleaning, science, dress-making, a bit of everything.
I still have my report here.
[LAUGHS] And I left that in '35.
Yeah.
I liked it.
Oh, my favorite subjects were reading and-- how do you call that?
[PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] About-- about the country and-- and--
Geography.
And about other countries, that was very big in Holland, more than here.
And I was quite good in that, so yeah, that was OK.
I wanted to be a salesgirl, because I was brought [LAUGHS] up in business.
But my mother didn't want that, so I became a dressmaker.
Very plain woman, very good little Yiddish mama.
That's all.
[LAUGHS]
My father was a real businessman and outgoing tip.
He liked to go out for dinner or for dancing or entertain people.
Was a completely different type of man.
No no.
His business, that was everything, the family.
No.
No.
No.
My mother had a miscarriage when I was, oh, maybe three years old and that was very bad.
And then she never wanted to have any more children, so I stayed on my own.
Good.
My father always made good money.
He was a hard worker.
So I was very spoiled, because I was alone.
And no, I was not short of anything.
Oh, I went on bike rides.
I went to gymnastics.
I went in an stage club.
That's why I'm on stage now.
[LAUGHTER] And so I had enough interests.
Not much, because my mother always was in the shop, so she couldn't take me on holidays.
Sometimes I went by myself, but that didn't work out very well.
To the beach.
Yeah.
Well, the Dutch Jews were very assimilated.
We mixed a lot.
There was not such an gap when you are not and you are.
That didn't-- didn't exist.
And that saved my life too.
So that was very good.
Yeah.
Well my uncle was also a wholesaler in textile.
That was in the whole family.
And he went to Germany to do business.
And there he saw the Hitlerjugend.
And that was I think '38.
And he came home distressed.
He didn't get out of his hotel room.
He was scared to go on the street.
And-- and then a lot of immigrants came from Germany and Poland to Holland and told the stories.
But we thought they exaggerate.
We couldn't believe that that happened, those pogroms, until it hit us.
And when Hitler came, the first year, year and a half, they didn't do anything.
They leave-- let us alone.
But there was a big mistake in Holland.
You had to be registered.
And there was an organization by the name of the Jews rat, Jewish council.
And everybody gave the name.
Stupid, but that's how it happened.
And you realized that later, of course.
And so when Hitler started, they know exactly where to go to pick up everybody.
That was [PAUSES FOR 6 SECONDS] I think the beginning of '42, end of '41 when it started.
First it was you had to give in your radio.
Then it was everything what was copper or metal.
And we did everything as long as they left us alone.
But they didn't leave us alone.
Then we had to wear the David Star.
No.
I don't know.
Also about the same period.
And that was disgusting.
But what could you do?
You couldn't do anything.
Everybody said, as long as they leave us alone.
Yeah.
Yeah.
Nothing.
It didn't bother you that much, because it was far from your bed, so to speak.
So you were not involved.
And yes, as I said, we couldn't believe the stories what came out, until we--
--were confronted with it.
Yes.
And there was a special camp in Holland, by the name of Westerbork.
It was in a-- like a [NON-ENGLISH].
And there, they interned those people.
Some of them walked free, maybe so many or whatever, I don't know.
But a lot of them were-- were in that camp.
But it was not a nasty camp.
They had little houses there.
And they could-- they were practically free to go, so it was no problem.
Yeah.
I was working.
Dressmaking.
Oh, for firm.
Big firm, yeah.
Yeah.
The first one was-- let me think.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] [INAUDIBLE].
[LAUGHS] Very funny name.
And the second one was-- oh, I don't know.
It was a German guy, but I can't remember his name.
Little German man.
OK.
No troubles.
So by that time I finished my apprenticeship, and [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] so I worked for him first as an apprentice.
And then my teacher had to go in divorce courts to the Hague.
And I was there on my own, so he said to me, you have to work for yourself now.
I said, oh, that's fine.
But when I worked for that woman, I made $2, 2 gilders 50, which was nothing.
And then that day when she was not there, I made 1 and 1/2 [INAUDIBLE].
And then she came back.
And I said, I'm not working for you anymore.
So it was a big upheaval, because that little girl there said she wasn't working anymore.
So the boss didn't know what to do.
And I said, if you don't let me work by myself, I'll leave.
So she had no choice and I worked there.
So that was that.
And then I-- then I got married.
I stopped, or maybe before I can't remember.
So long ago.
Heyman Waas.
And we planned a big wedding, because I was an only child.
And even so, we didn't go to synagogue.
My father hired some people from the choir from the synagogue.
And we hired a hall and music.
Everything was set.
But then they started to pick up boys.
And everybody said it was too dangerous, because if so many men are together, they come in and pick all the boys out and take them away.
The Germans.
And I got married I think the 3rd of July.
And the 10 or something like that they started to pick up boys from the street.
And we were in the city to buy something, that was my first husband and my second husband and his wife and their baby.
Karel and--
Karel Abram.
And-- and the-- and his parents were--
Bluma Abram and Philip Abram.
And we came out of the city and we were warned through Jewish people, go back, because in the next street they pick the men up.
And we were desperate.
We didn't know what to do.
We didn't have a clue what was going on.
And we hide somewhere.
But the baby was in a pram, so we were stuck with the pram.
We didn't have food for the baby.
We didn't have nappies, nothing.
So then my husband or what was then my friend said, across the street where they were picking up the boys, there is a guy I worked for, because he learned to be [INAUDIBLE] building.
And he said, I'll go to those people when it's quiet and ask them if they'll keep the pram.
And so we did.
And then we picked up a cab and went to my mother's-in-law place.
And there we put towels on the baby, because we didn't have nappies.
And I stayed 'til it was dark, and then they went home to their place and I went to my place.
And-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] and then I decided I'm not going to wait to be picked up.
So I said, I'll go in hiding.
With my first husband, yeah.
But he worked for a while for my father.
And he had red hair, so from a mile away you could see him.
And he worked in Limburg, that is south of Holland, of Amsterdam so to speak.
And he went there, did some traveling.
And people knew him, because he was a representative for the firm.
And then when we fled, I went to a customer, but they were Jewish people too.
And they had two boys.
And she said, OK.
I'll take you for a few days, but you can't stay here, because we don't know what happen to us.
So we stayed there for a couple of days.
In the meantime, my father made contact with somebody who lived in that area.
And they were supposed to give us a hiding place.
And I was three months pregnant for my son, my middle son.
That was working for the underground movement.
That I don't know.
Can't remember.
And then she said to us, I've got an address.
But first, in lunchtime, you go to that and that street, that and that people, and stay there 'til the night.
And in the nighttime, we will pick you up and bring you to a permanent address.
So like I had an instinct that something was not OK.
So I said to my husband, you walk one side of the street and I walk the other.
And so we did.
And then we came to a corner and there were maybe six people from the village, what you could see real village people.
And they were standing there talking.
And I walked on the right side and he walked on the left.
And they stopped him.
But we were walking without a star, without the David Star, and no papers.
We were not allowed to travel in a train or anything.
So they stopped him and asked him for his papers, which he didn't have.
So they took him away.
And I went to the people I was supposed to go to and I said, I'm going to give myself up and I'm not staying here and I [INAUDIBLE] myself in.
And they said no, you've got a baby and you're not.
Anyway, half an hour later, the military police came.
Yeah.
Sittard.
No.
No.
No.
The-- the-- the customer was-- I can't remember what the name was.
Very nice people.
I've got a photo in the kitchen from it.
Anyway, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the military police took me away and brought me to the military place where they stayed.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And I didn't know what to do.
And in the night, I don't know how many times I called them, then I had an excuse, a bit of water, then I had to go to the toilet, any excuse to talk.
But there was no talking.
And then the next morning, they took us to another town, Maastricht.
The military police and my husband, because they put him in another cell.
And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] then we came in in Maastricht, and there was the jail.
And he was put in the men's section and I was put in the woman's.
And I stayed there for 12 days with two other people in a cell what was original for one woman, for one person.
And--
Yeah.
Dutch borders, yeah.
And then after 12 days--
Oh, yes.
They gave you food.
But you have to-- to live in a little space what actually was for one person.
That was one lady who was caught at the border.
She wanted to go over the border.
And the other one was not Jewish at all, but married to a Jew.
And they were musicians and they played in Maastricht music for a living.
And somebody [INAUDIBLE] them in because he was Jewish.
And she was brought in too.
So we were there with the three of us.
Then the 12 days, the warden came and said to me, you have to go to the white house.
And the white house was where the SS was.
But before it all started that I left Amsterdam and all that, I said to my husband, if we are caught, I'll take the blame.
I'll say I'm pregnant and I'm nervous wreck and whatever.
So we kept to that agreement.
Then when I came outside, there was a policeman with a motorcycle with the side [INAUDIBLE], a sidecar.
And I had to put up a star, a David Star.
And there I went in the sidecar to [LAUGHS] the white house.
And when I came there, there were three men there, three German.
And they asked me I don't know how many questions, what I was doing and why I was there and all that business.
And I should know I was not allowed to travel without papers and without a star and all that.
[SIGHS] How do you feel?
So I made all kind of excuses and they didn't say much.
And then but when I went in that motorcycle, the policeman said to me, you must have regards for your husband.
I said, how come?
He said, he went this morning.
And he want me to tell you that he's OK.
All right.
So then they drove me back.
And when I came back in the cell, then they-- after maybe 5 or 10 minutes the warden came back.
And she said, you can go.
But I didn't believe it, because in those days, they said that to people to tease them and then they didn't let them go.
So I was scared for that.
And I said, are you having me on?
No, you're free to go.
But tonight you have to sleep with somebody from the Jewish Council.
And they get you papers to travel.
OK.
So I slept with those people.
Do you remember their names?
No.
No idea.
Never saw him in my life.
Anyway, then I came in Amsterdam.
And I--
By train.
I had special papers.
And somebody warned my father that I was free.
So--
That was-- I left in July.
And so I was 12 days there.
And then when I came back, I didn't want to go in my own flat, because I didn't feel safe.
But I had to go to a doctor to get a certificate that I was not able to look after myself and that I was pregnant and that I have to have permission to stay with my parents.
So I got a letter from a doctor and I stayed with my parents.
Yeah.
